sectionTitle: A century of gathering, made new
lead: Built in 1921 and designed by the celebrated Iowa firm Proudfoot, Bird & Rawson, this building educated generations of Jefferson students before its doors closed in 2020. Today it begins a new chapter — as a place to call home.
paragraphs: For nearly a hundred years, this four-story landmark stood at the center of community life in Jefferson, serving first as the high school and later as the middle school. When the bell rang for the last time, developer Chris Deal saw not an empty building, but a future.

The Jeffersonian is a careful adaptive-reuse restoration that converts the historic schoolhouse into 25 apartments while preserving the details that make it irreplaceable. Original hallway lockers remain in place, large windows have been reinstated to flood rooms with light, and wood cupboards, doorframes, and shelving have been woven into many of the homes.

Carried out by Woodruff Construction and supported by nearly $2.5 million in historic preservation tax credits, the roughly $8.5 million project earned developer Chris Deal a 2025 Housing Impact Award from the Ames Regional Economic Alliance. It is a model for how small towns can honor their past while building for their future.

highlights.title: Designed by Proudfoot, Bird & Rawson
highlights.body: The same renowned firm behind landmark buildings across Iowa shaped this 1921 schoolhouse — and its bones still anchor the design today.

highlights.title: Preserved, not erased
highlights.body: Hallway lockers, restored windows, and original woodwork are kept as living history rather than swept away.

highlights.title: For neighbors of every chapter
highlights.body: Thoughtfully geared toward retirees and young professionals alike, with at least five homes set aside as income-qualified housing.
